posts.text: Heads up weirdos - it's our time to shine! According to Allie K. Miller - if you want real results from AI, find your weirdos. I'm happy to agree. And not just because I'm one. What does she mean by "weirdos?" The curious ones. The compulsive tinkerers. The people who push a tool until it breaks, then push harder. While many get stuck using AI at 5-10% of its potential, the weirdos are well past that. Not because they're more technical, but because they can't help but to wonder and experiment. Here's the part TA and HR should seriously think about...there's a very real chance that some of our hiring processes (and people) screen these people OUT, overlook their potential, and fail to reward them with recognition and advancement, whether consciously or unconsciously. Too different. Doesn't fit the mold. Hard to put in a box. Not like me/us (affinity bias, anyone???). Weird. Well, AI rewards weirdos. And here's why. "Standard" software rewards knowing the one right way to do a thing. Learn it once, and you were "good at it." AI is the opposite. No manual. No single correct input. No "right way" to hold it. No limit to what you can do with it. The whole game is the loop - try something, read what comes back, adjust, try again. Then try the strange thing you're a little embarrassed to ask out loud. That loop is miserable for people who need to be right on the first try and for those who find uncertainty and not knowing uncomfortable. For weirdos, it's a playground. We were running it in our heads long before the tools showed up. So a question worth asking inside your own org and company - who are your weirdos? Do you even know? And are you supporting them and amplifying their ability to impact and transform your group or company, or quietly overlooking them or politely or unconsciously managing them out? I myself, am strange and unusual. For most of my career, being the "weirdo" in the room was something to manage. I have no doubt that my nature has been instrumental in the things I've figured out and contributed to in my career, but I can also say that it's not always safe or comfortable being the weirdo. And I am absolutely certain I am not alone. The good news? AI may give us a greater opportunity than we've ever had to make a difference to ourselves, to our companies, and to the world. #stayweird
posts.hook: Heads up weirdos - it's our time to shine!
posts.name: Glen Cathey

posts.text: The US Department of Labor has shipped three solid resources every TA and HR leader hiring in the US should know about. Over the past few months, the DOL has launched real artifacts as part of an effort to prepare America for the AI transformation of work: → The AI Literacy Framework (Feb 2026): five foundational content areas, seven delivery principles. Voluntary - but voluntary federal frameworks have a way of becoming benchmark expectations in audits, in lawsuits, in RFPs. Check it out - it's solid! → Make America AI-Ready (March 2026): a free, text-based, 7-day AI course - about 10 minutes a day. Text READY to 20202. No app, no laptop, no internet required. I've taken it myself - it's especially helpful for anyone who isn't already an AI power user. → The AI in Registered Apprenticeship Innovation Portal (April 2026): tools to integrate AI skills into new or existing apprenticeship programs, with industry-specific modules. Then layer in what Acting Labor Secretary (and former EEOC Commissioner) Keith Sonderling has said about AI in hiring: "Using these tools, working with these developers, we may have the most transparent employment decisions ever because you can show exactly what went in the algorithm... But at the end of the day, the employer is still making that decision." That's the entire Automated Decision Making (ADM) compliance line in one sentence. Algorithmic inputs can be traced - but traceability only mitigates risk if you've actually documented your inputs, your Meaningful Human Involvement, and your override logic. What % of TA functions have done this? Your AI hiring stack will increasingly be evaluated against an emerging federal vocabulary, and free practitioner resources are already on the shelf. I'll share links in the comments. Have any of you used the framework, the AI course, or the apprenticeship portal yet? If you haven't - check them out.
posts.hook: The US Department of Labor has shipped three solid resources every TA and HR leader hiring in the US should know about.
posts.name: Glen Cathey

posts.text: Is customer service actually bounded? This is the more interesting failure, and it generates the real insight. The volume of inquiries is bounded. But quality is an unbounded dimension hiding inside the same role. Freed capacity can go into proactive outreach, retention, turning support from a cost center into a loyalty and revenue function. Which means bounded work can sometimes be converted into boundless work - by redefining the job around the open-ended dimension (the relationship, the prevention, the experience) instead of the capped one (tickets closed per hour).
posts.name: Glen Cathey

posts.text: When leaders mistake "I can vividly picture the layoffs and can't picture the new roles" for "the future is contraction," they make contractionary decisions. They stop building. They stop developing people. They optimize for a shrinking world. And that becomes self-fulfilling - the talent version of a doom loop, where the prediction quietly creates the conditions that prove it right.
posts.hook: When leaders mistake "I can vividly picture the layoffs and can't picture the new roles" for "the future is contraction," they make contract…
posts.name: Glen Cathey

posts.text: And here is the part that makes this so easy to miss - this may not come from any conscious strategy at all. No one has to issue a memo cutting junior headcount. It can happen when a mid-level or senior person, with no strategy in mind, simply uses AI to knock out the first-draft research, the cleanup, the analysis they would once have handed down. The work still gets done. It just never becomes a reason to hire. The latent demand that used to spill into an entry-level req quietly evaporates - not by design. Which is exactly why it may not surface in anyone's workforce plan until the pipeline is already thin.
posts.hook: And here is the part that makes this so easy to miss - this may not come from any conscious strategy at all.
posts.name: Glen Cathey

posts.text: The second version is also real. It's being built right now, by people who took the criticism seriously and designed around it. It's a fool's errand to try and make everyone happy (typically impossible), but if we let the loudest objections kill the entire experiment, we don't get a return to a human-first hiring process that never actually existed at the volumes we're operating at. We get the same broken process, just slower, with more ghosting and fewer chances for candidates to be seen at all.
posts.hook: The second version is also real.
posts.name: Glen Cathey

posts.text: I'll add my own experience here, because it's relevant. I've spent the majority of my nearly thirty years now in talent acquisition building and managing teams and training people in sourcing and recruiting - often people who came to my teams with no prior sourcing or recruiting experience whatsoever. Teaching from scratch, repeatedly, across decades, has not diminished my sourcing and recruiting judgment. It has deepened it, and I've often said it feels like "unfair advantage" I would not have gained had I not moved into leadership and had the opportunity to develop so many people in this domain. Every time I've had to articulate the why behind something to someone learning, I've had to re-examine and refine my own understanding of what actually works. The act of explanation, repeated across hundreds of practitioners and thousands of situations, has built and amplified the judgment I bring to the work today. I'd argue I have more accurate and useful sourcing and recruiting wisdom now than I had when I was personally doing it full-time, precisely because the required metacognition and coaching forced me to make tacit domain expertise explicit. (A separate observation worth its own article: the people I trained from scratch, with no prior experience, often outperformed those who came to my teams with prior recruiting backgrounds. That pattern is interesting enough that I'll write about it elsewhere.)
posts.hook: I'll add my own experience here, because it's relevant.
posts.name: Glen Cathey

posts.text: Also in my first 6 months, when my only source of candidates was an internal Lotus Notes ATS/database (before I used the internet, online job boards, and other sources) - when working challenging jobs I would often hit a wall. I had searched what felt like the entire internal database for hard-to-fill roles and I often wouldn't find anyone viable. However, I still had to produce at least 2 QIA candidates. So I wondered...
posts.hook: Also in my first 6 months, when my only source of candidates was an internal Lotus Notes ATS/database (before I used the internet, online jo…
posts.name: Glen Cathey

posts.text: If AI agents are advancing at this pace, what does the recruiter role look like in 2028? Agent accuracy on real computer tasks went from 12% to 66% in one year. If that trajectory continues, significant portions of the recruiting workflow - sourcing, screening, scheduling, even initial outreach - could be handled by agents within a few years (where allowed). The question isn't whether that happens. It's whether recruiters are developing the skills that AI agents can't replicate - judgment, relationship depth, strategic advisory capability.
posts.hook: If AI agents are advancing at this pace, what does the recruiter role look like in 2028?
posts.name: Glen Cathey

posts.text: 4.  Protect the pipeline. If you're reducing or freezing entry-level hiring, you need an explicit strategy for how you'll develop the next generation of experienced workers. Apprenticeship models, structured mentorship, rotational programs - something. Because the market will not provide you with experienced talent in five years if nobody is developing them today, although an experienced talent shortage in the future could be a boon to experienced recruiters when this hits.
posts.name: Glen Cathey
